title: St. Antipas of Pergamum, St. Pharmuthius & St. Barsanuphius of Palestine
narrative:
title": "St. Antipas of Pergamum, St. Pharmuthius & St. Barsanuphius of Palestine

narrative": "title\": \"St. Antipas of Pergamum, St. Pharmuthius & St. Barsanuphius of Palestine

narrative\": \"St. Antipas of Pergamum the Martyr, St. Pharmuthius the Hermit, and St. Barsanuphius the Great of Palestine

On the eleventh of Miazia, the holy Church sets before the faithful three witnesses whose relationship to the written word is the thread that unites them: the bishop whose name appears in the book that ends scripture, the hermit whose sayings were written on leaves, and the great spiritual father whose letters filled volumes. Saint Antipas of Pergamum is the most ancient figure of this assembly and one of the most scripturally attested: named in the book of Revelation as my faithful witness, who was killed among you, where Satan dwells, he is the martyr whose blood was shed in the city where the great throne of Zeus stood — the throne that tradition identified with the seat of Satan — and whose name the risen Christ preserved by personal attestation in the letters to the seven churches. To be mentioned by name by the glorified Christ in the text of the Revelation is a distinction shared by almost no one in the entire canon of saints, and the tradition has always treated Antipas's brief scriptural mention as the equivalent of a full hagiography.

Saint Pharmuthius is a hermit of the Egyptian desert whose tradition has a particular quality of gentle eccentricity that the Apophthegmata tradition most prizes: a man who lived alone in the deepest desert and who, the tradition records, was known to leave small gifts of food or necessary items at the doors of other hermits' cells without ever announcing himself — anonymous charity in the most literal sense, gifts that arrived without explanation and whose giver was known only to God. He is said to have had a particular tenderness for the sick and for those in spiritual crisis, visiting them in disguise so that they could not thank him or become dependent on him, and withdrawing before his identity could be established. In the dawn of Miazia, Pharmuthius embodies the charity of the Resurrection that arrives without announcement, that gives without waiting to be acknowledged, that transforms situations that seemed hopeless without requiring the credit for the transformation.

Saint Barsanuphius the Great of Palestine is one of the supreme figures of the Eastern spiritual tradition: the recluse of the monastery of Seridos near Gaza who spent decades in his cell writing letters — hundreds of them, preserved and transmitted by his disciple John the Prophet — that addressed the practical and interior needs of monks, laypeople, bishops, and emperors with equal depth and equal pastoral precision. His letters on discernment, on prayer, on the passions, on the relationship between the spiritual father and the disciple, constitute one of the most complete and practically useful bodies of spiritual direction in the entire patristic tradition. He never left his cell, never met most of those he advised, and yet his influence was felt across the entire Byzantine world. Barsanuphius is the Miazia dawn's image of the risen Christ's own mode of presence: unseen, yet completely attentive; absent from sight, yet more present than those who are visible; communicating through letters, as the risen Lord communicated through the scriptures that burned in the disciples' hearts on the road to Emmaus. Amen.
